start.title: Quick start
start.intro: Nexus is an infrastructure management platform for monitoring, updating, and administering your Ubuntu/Debian servers from a single web interface.
start.trustWarn: One instance = a single trust domain (no per-user or per-tenant isolation). Before sharing an instance across multiple teams or clients, read the Security section: for separate trust domains, deploy separate instances.
start.archServer: Nexus server — Fastify backend + React frontend, deployed with Docker Compose
start.archAgent: Nexus agent — Go binary installed on each managed machine (systemd service)
start.prereq1: Docker + Docker Compose on the Nexus server
start.prereq2: Ubuntu 20.04+ or Debian 11+ on the target machines
start.prereq3: Network access between the machines and the Nexus server (port 26033)
start.installCode:
# Clone the project
git clone <repo> nexus && cd nexus

# Configure the environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with your values (JWT_SECRET, ECDSA_MASTER_SECRET, etc.)

# Start it
docker compose up -d

# The dashboard is available at:
#   https://localhost:26033  (self-signed HTTPS)
#   http://localhost:26032   (redirects to HTTPS)
start.firstLoginText: Sign in with the admin account created by the database seed. Change the password after your first login.
start.firstMachineText: From the Dashboard or the Machines page, click "Add a machine". Nexus generates a unique enrollment token valid for 24h. Use this token to install the agent on the target machine.
start.bootstrapWarn: Mandatory bootstrap order. The agent binary is not bundled in the backend image: it is served from the release/ volume, populated only by a signed release. Until a release is published, the download returns an error ("binary not available") — no agent install or update is possible. Correct order: deploy the backend → publish the first signed release → then install agents. (Already-enrolled agents are unaffected.)

agent.title: Agent installation
agent.intro: The Nexus agent is a lightweight Go binary that installs as a systemd service on each machine you manage.
agent.step1Text: In the web interface, go to Machines → Add a machine. Pick a name. Nexus gives you:
agent.step1Key: Backend Public Key — The server's public key
agent.step2Title: 2. Install the agent with the script
agent.scriptCode:
# On the target machine (as root)
sudo bash install-agent.sh \
  --server-url wss://nexus-server:26033/ws/agent \
  --machine-id cmxxxxxxxxx \
  --enrollment-token enroll_xxxxxxxxxxxx \
  --server-public-key "-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----..."
agent.scriptStep4: Installs and starts the systemd service
agent.manualCode:
# Copy the binary
sudo cp nexus-agent /usr/local/bin/
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/nexus-agent

# Create the config
sudo mkdir -p /etc/nexus /opt/nexus/keys
sudo tee /etc/nexus/agent.env << EOF
NEXUS_SERVER_URL=wss://nexus-server:26033/ws/agent
NEXUS_MACHINE_ID=<machine-id>
NEXUS_ENROLLMENT_TOKEN=<token>
NEXUS_KEY_PATH=/opt/nexus/keys
NEXUS_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL=30
NEXUS_METRICS_INTERVAL=60
EOF

# Install the systemd service
sudo cp nexus-agent.service /etc/systemd/system/
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now nexus-agent

# Verify
sudo systemctl status nexus-agent
sudo journalctl -u nexus-agent -f
agent.step4Item1: Connects to the server over WebSocket (WSS)
agent.step4Item4: The machine switches to ONLINE status in the dashboard
agent.enrollTip: Enrollment runs automatically on first startup. Keys are stored in /opt/nexus/keys/ and reused on later startups.
agent.capsText: An enrolled agent has full access: metrics, updates, services, firewall, netplan, users, packages, reboot, and more. What each user can actually trigger depends on their role (ADMIN/OPERATOR/READONLY) and the "critical machine" flag. The agent's root capabilities are defined by the sudoers generated at install time.
agent.criticalText: In Settings, check ⚠ Critical machine for sensitive machines (Nexus server, prod DB, etc.). This blocks:
agent.criticalItem2: <c>service_stop/restart</c> on docker, nginx, ssh, postgresql, traefik, keycloak
agent.criticalItem3: <c>package.remove</c> on docker-ce, nginx, postgresql, openssh-*, systemd, sudo, apt
agent.reinstallText: When a new Nexus version adds sudo rules (new agent actions), existing agents keep their old sudoers and the new actions fail. A ⚠ Outdated sudoers badge appears on the machine. To resync:
agent.reinstallCode:
# 1. SSH into the affected machine
ssh user@machine-ip

# 2. Re-run the script with the same parameters as the initial install.
#    The enrollment token is NOT required: the agent reuses its existing
#    ECDSA keys (/var/lib/nexus/keys/) and does not re-enroll. The script
#    only refreshes the sudoers and the binary.
sudo bash install-agent.sh \
  --server-url wss://nexus.example.com/ws/agent \
  --machine-id <machine-id>

# 3. Restart the service so the agent reloads the sudoers hash
sudo systemctl restart nexus-agent
agent.reinstallAfter: On the next heartbeat (~30s), the badge clears automatically.
agent.uninstallText: The script now handles a full uninstall via --uninstall (alias --purge). It removes the service, the binary, the ECDSA keys, the shared secret, the config, the watchdog snapshots, the sudoers, the system user, and removes it from the systemd-journal group:
agent.uninstallCode:
# On the target machine, as root
sudo /tmp/install-agent.sh --uninstall

# (the script is also in /opt/nexus if you kept it)
agent.uninstallWarn: --uninstall also deletes the ECDSA keys and the shared secret. To re-enroll afterward, use the Re-enroll button in the UI (the old keys are no longer valid).
agent.reenrollText: To start over cleanly (keys regenerated on the backend AND residual state purged on the machine — the cause of re-enrollment deadlocks):
agent.reenrollStep1: In the Nexus UI → the machine → Re-enroll button. This regenerates a token + a new backend ECDSA keypair, disconnects the agent, and invalidates the old install tokens.
agent.reenrollStep2: Copy the new install command shown: it contains --reenroll, which automatically purges the residual identity (keys, shared secret, old server key, watchdog snapshots) before re-enrolling.
agent.reenrollStep3: Run it on the machine. No manual uninstall is needed beforehand.
agent.reenrollCli: Command-line equivalent without the UI: <c>install-agent.sh --uninstall</c> then a fresh install — or directly <c>install-agent.sh --reenroll ...</c> with the new token.

self.title: Self-monitoring — Install the agent on the Nexus server
self.intro: To monitor the Nexus server itself (metrics, services, disk, updates), you can install a Nexus agent on the machine that hosts Nexus. The agent runs natively on the host (not in a container) and reports to the local Nexus instance.
self.dogfoodTip: Dogfooding: lets you catch a Nexus server under strain (CPU maxed out, disk full, container restart) and receive alerts. If Nexus goes down entirely, you'll see nothing — that's an expected limitation.
self.step1Text: In the Nexus UI → Machines → Add a machine. Suggested name: <c>nexus-server</c>.
self.step2Title: 2. Run the install command on the host
self.step2Text: Copy the command provided by the UI and run it directly on the host (not in a container). The agent connects to Nexus via <c>ws://localhost:26031/ws/agent</c> or the public domain name, depending on your config.
self.dockerWarn: Docker edge case: if Nexus runs behind a reverse proxy (nginx/traefik), use the public URL in --server-url. Otherwise, make sure the backend is exposed on localhost:26031.
self.step3Text: The <c>nexus-server</c> machine shows up as <on>ONLINE</on> within a few seconds. You'll see:
self.alertsTitle: Recommended alerts for the Nexus server
self.alert1: DISK_ABOVE 85% on nexus-server — keeps Docker logs / Postgres volumes from filling the disk
self.alert3: SERVICE_FAILED "docker" — if Docker goes down, all of Nexus goes down
self.alert4: CERT_EXPIRING 14 days — for the public reverse proxy cert
self.alertsAfter: These alerts go through the standard webhook/email channels. If Nexus fails completely, they won't fire — which is why running an external monitor like uptime-kuma alongside is worthwhile.

machines.title: Machine management
machines.intro: The Machines page lists every registered machine with its real-time status.
machines.statusOnline: ONLINE — The agent is connected and sending data
machines.statusPending: ENROLLMENT_PENDING — Machine created, waiting for the agent to connect
machines.statusRevoked: REVOKED — Keys revoked, the agent can no longer connect
machines.lifecycleText: Inactive machines follow an automatic cycle (delays configurable in Settings):
machines.lifecycleCode: OFFLINE → (7 days) → STALE → (30 days) → ARCHIVED → (90 days) → Deleted
machines.tabsText: On a machine's detail page (click a card), 14 tabs grouped into 5 categories:
machines.tabOverview: Overview — System info, network, editable settings, SSL certs
machines.tabSystem: System — systemd services (list + logs + start/stop/restart), Scheduled tasks (timers + cron + toggle), Users (CRUD + SSH keys)
machines.tabNetwork: Network — Interfaces, Netplan (YAML editor + 120s watchdog), ufw firewall (allow/deny + 60s watchdog)
machines.tabSoftware: Software — Updates (apt upgrade + package hold/unhold), Packages (FTS search + install/remove)
machines.bulkText: On the Machines page, check several machines to reveal the Bulk action (N) button. You can run in parallel across up to 100 machines:
machines.bulkAfter: Watchdog-revert actions (netplan, firewall) stay per-machine (confirmation required).
machines.deleteText: The context menu (⋮) on each machine card lets you revoke or delete. Revoking invalidates the agent's keys — it cannot reconnect without re-enrollment.

tags.title: Tags & Groups
tags.intro: Tags help you organize your machines (e.g. "prod", "web", "db"). Groups let you bundle machines together for bulk actions.
tags.tagsText: Create tags from the Tags page. Each tag has a name and a color. Assign tags to machines from the machine's detail page.
tags.tagsTip: Tags are used by Profiles to target the machines that automatic actions run on.
tags.groupStatic: Static — You add and remove machines manually
tags.groupDynamic: Dynamic — Machines are resolved automatically from filters (tags, status)

alerts.title: Alerts & Notifications
alerts.intro: Set up alert rules to get notified when a machine crosses a threshold or becomes unreachable.
alerts.healthHeading: System health (evaluated every 5 min, polls the agent)
alerts.condService: SERVICE_FAILED — A systemd service has failed. Optional name filter (e.g. "nginx", "postgres").
alerts.condTimer: TIMER_FAILED — A systemd timer whose triggered service has failed.
alerts.condCron: CRON_FAILED — A cron job has failed (in preparation).
alerts.condUpdates: UPDATES_AVAILABLE — More than N apt updates available (threshold = N).
alerts.condCert: CERT_EXPIRING — At least one SSL cert expires within ≤ N days (threshold = N).
alerts.condHardening: HARDENING_INDEX_BELOW — The hardening index (Lynis) from the latest audit drops below N/100 (threshold = N). Evaluated against the last persisted scan + on every new audit (Security tab).
alerts.notifWebhook: Webhook — HMAC-SHA256-signed HTTP POST to a URL of your choice
alerts.notifEmail: Email — Over SMTP (Gmail supported). Configure it in Settings.
alerts.smtpCode:
Host: smtp.gmail.com
Port: 587
User: your.email@gmail.com
Password: (Google app password)
From: your.email@gmail.com
alerts.smtpTip: For Gmail, you need to create an "app password" in your Google account's security settings.

updates.title: System updates
updates.intro: Nexus can check for and install system updates on your Ubuntu/Debian machines.
updates.checkText: From a machine's detail page → "Updates" tab → "Check updates". The agent runs <c>apt-get update</c> and returns the list of packages to update.
updates.bulkText: On the Machines page, select several machines with the checkboxes, then click Bulk action → System update. Dispatched in parallel with concurrency capped at 10 machines. Results are consolidated in a table (OK / Failed / Skipped).
updates.bulkAlt: Alternatively, Profiles let you schedule updates with staggered delivery and tag-based targeting.
updates.pinText: In the Software → Updates tab, every upgradable package has a lock icon in the Hold column:
updates.pinOpen: Open lock (gray): the package will follow the next upgrade.
updates.pinClosed: Closed lock (orange): package "held" via apt-mark hold, will NOT be upgraded.
updates.pinUse: Use cases: hold a specific kernel until you've validated a reboot, or pin postgresql to an LTS version.
updates.rebootWarn: Updates run as root on the target machine. A reboot may be required after some updates (kernel). A "Reboot required" indicator will appear on the machine.

ssh.title: SSH configuration
ssh.intro: The SSH button on a machine's page uses the ssh:// scheme to open your local terminal already connected. This scheme isn't set up by default on modern operating systems. Here's how to enable it.
ssh.copyTip: You can always copy the <c>ssh user@ip</c> command and paste it into your terminal by hand, with no setup.
ssh.macText: Terminal.app hasn't been the default <c>ssh://</c> handler since macOS 10.6. The easiest way to re-enable it:
ssh.macCode:
brew install --cask swiftdefaultappsprefpane
# Then System Settings → SwiftDefaultApps → URL Schemes
# Associate "ssh" with Terminal.app (or iTerm2, Warp...)
ssh.linuxText: Create a <c>.desktop</c> file that declares the handler:
ssh.linuxEmulator: Replace <c>gnome-terminal --</c> with your own emulator if needed:
ssh.wslText: If you use WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux), the simplest approach is to route ssh:// to wsl ssh. Create a nexus-ssh-wsl.reg file:
ssh.wslAfter: Double-click the file and confirm the import. The WSL terminal will now open with the SSH connection already established.
ssh.wtTip: For better rendering in Windows Terminal (default WSL profile):
ssh.noWslText: Native OpenSSH (included in Windows 10+). Create a .reg file that uses wt.exe:
ssh.noWslAfter: Requires Windows Terminal 1.16+ and <c>Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name OpenSSH.Client~~~~0.0.1.0</c> if the SSH client isn't installed.
ssh.puttyText: During PuTTY installation, check "associate with ssh:// URLs". Simple, but the result looks worse than WSL/Windows Terminal.
ssh.userText: On a machine's Overview page, the Settings card lets you set a default SSH user (e.g. root, ubuntu). It will be pre-filled automatically in the SSH button for that machine.

security.title: Security
security.trustWarn: One Nexus instance = a single trust domain. There is no isolation by user or tenant: any OPERATOR can act on all machines, any READONLY can read all machines. Don't deploy a shared instance for teams or clients that don't mutually trust each other — use separate instances.
security.intro: Nexus uses several layers of security to protect communications and actions.
security.cryptoEcdsa: ECDSA P-256 — Signs every agent ↔ server message
security.cryptoEcdh: ECDHE X25519 — Ephemeral handshake (forward secrecy) that establishes the session secret at runtime; enrollment establishes identity only (sealed against the pinned server key), no session secret is derived there.
security.antiReplayText: Every message carries a unique nonce and a timestamp. Nonces are kept in an LRU cache (5-min TTL) to detect replay attempts.
security.authWs: Dashboard WebSocket — JWT token verified when the WS connection opens
security.roleAdmin: ADMIN — Full access (create/delete machines, profiles, tags, etc.)

supplychain.title: Agent build, signing & verification
supplychain.intro: Nexus upgrades its own agents in place. The trust root for that upgrade is an offline signing key held by the operator — not the backend. This page explains how the agent binary is built, signed, and verified before it ever replaces a running agent.
supplychain.property: A compromised backend cannot push a trojaned binary. It serves the binary and relays its signature, but never holds the offline private key — so an agent only installs an upgrade whose signature verifies against a public key you deployed locally.
supplychain.buildText: The agent is a static Go binary built with CGO_ENABLED=0, -trimpath and a Go toolchain pinned by digest. This makes the build byte-for-byte reproducible: rebuilding the same source yields the same sha256, wherever you build it.
supplychain.buildCode:
# Rebuild the agent from source with the pinned toolchain
docker run --rm -v "$PWD/agent:/src:ro" \
  golang:1.23-alpine@sha256:383395b794dffa5b53012a212365d40c8e37109a626ca30d6151c8348d380b5f \
  sh -c 'cp -r /src /b && cd /b && CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 \
    go build -trimpath -mod=readonly \
    -ldflags "-w -s -X main.Version=<RELEASE_VERSION>" \
    -o /tmp/nexus-agent ./cmd/nexus-agent && sha256sum /tmp/nexus-agent'
supplychain.buildCompare: Use the exact <c>RELEASE_VERSION</c> shipped with the release (the version string is baked into the binary). The resulting <c>sha256</c> must match the one published next to the signed binary.
supplychain.signText: Each release is signed with a minisign (Ed25519) key that never leaves an offline machine. A human reviews the version and sha256, then signs — producing a detached .minisig published next to the binary. The backend only relays this signature.
supplychain.verifyText: Before replacing itself, the agent runs a strict, fail-closed check chain:
supplychain.verify1: Signature — the detached minisign signature must verify against the local accept-list at /etc/nexus/release.pub (this is the authority; missing or empty ⇒ refused).
supplychain.verify3: Anti-rollback — the target version must not be older than the running one (unless explicitly allowed).
supplychain.verify4: Origin pinning + anti-TOCTOU — the download only comes from the pinned backend over https, and the binary is re-checked right before install and restart.
supplychain.trustConvenience: Convenience — trust the project's signed release (like a signed apt package). Verify it yourself via the reproducible build above.
supplychain.trustAssurance: High assurance — generate your own release key so the upgrade trust root is yours alone. Recommended for isolated deployments.
supplychain.keysTip: Key generation, deployment and rotation are documented in <c>OPERATOR-KEYS.md</c> (release key, script-signing key, server identity key).
